questions.question: Hot-rolled bar shows tight, longitudinal lines of nonmetallic material strung out along the working direction. These stringers originated:
options: During final machining of the bar
As inclusions in the ingot, elongated during rolling — an inherent discontinuity
questions.explanation: Stringers are nonmetallic inclusions from the original melt drawn out along the working direction. Because their origin is solidification, they are classed as inherent even though rolling gave them their shape.

questions.question: Cracks discovered at the sharp corners of a part immediately after rapid quenching from heat treatment are classed as:
questions.explanation: Heat treatment is a secondary processing operation, and quench cracks at sharp corners and section changes are its signature discontinuity — the part never saw service.

questions.question: A concrete beam shows rust staining with fragments of the surface breaking away. As a VT application on a mineral-based material, this most likely indicates:
options: Corrosion of the embedded reinforcing steel causing spalling
A cold shut from the original pour
questions.explanation: Corroding rebar expands and pops the concrete cover off — spalling — and the rust staining betrays the cause. Efflorescence is a white deposit, and cold shuts belong to metal castings.

questions.question: Along the trimmed edge of a fiberglass panel you see plies separating, with a light, fuzzy zone between the layers. This organic-based material discontinuity is:
questions.explanation: Layer separation in a laminated composite is delamination, and trimmed edges and drilled holes are exactly where it starts. Segregation and seams belong to metals; spalling belongs to concrete and ceramics.

questions.question: The first part off a newly set up forming die receives a detailed examination before the production run is allowed to continue. This sampling approach is called:
questions.explanation: First-article inspection examines the first piece from a new setup, die, or process in detail, catching setup errors while the defective count is still one part.

questions.question: For direct visual examination, widely used code practice places the eye:
options: Within 12 in. of the surface, at any viewing angle
Within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface, at an angle not less than 30 degrees to it
Within 36 in. of the surface, at an angle not less than 45 degrees to it
At any distance, provided lighting exceeds 500 lux
questions.explanation: The classic direct-viewing envelope is eye within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface and a viewing angle of not less than 30 degrees to the surface — flatter angles and longer distances degrade detection.

questions.question: Before examining a large tank shell, the examiner chalks the surface into numbered blocks and signs each block off as it is completed. The purpose is to:
options: Estimate the amount of paint required for repairs
Guarantee and document complete, systematic coverage of the surface
Divide the work fairly among several shifts
Improve the lighting within each block
questions.explanation: Grid segmentation turns a big surface into provable coverage: each block is scanned in a controlled pattern and checked off, so interruptions never create unexamined gaps.

questions.question: At the job site you find the light meter's calibration sticker expired last month, and the procedure requires verifying 1,000 lux at the surface. You should:
options: Use the meter anyway — it probably still reads correctly
Estimate the lighting by eye, since the lamps look bright
Stop: the reading cannot verify the requirement, so report it and obtain a calibrated meter
Apply 500 lux as the requirement instead, to add margin
questions.explanation: Verification requires a traceable, in-calibration instrument; an expired meter proves nothing. Standardization exists precisely to keep examinations from proceeding under unverified conditions.

questions.question: Place these steps in the correct order: (1) evaluate against acceptance criteria, (2) detect an indication, (3) report the result, (4) interpret the indication.
questions.explanation: Detection produces an indication; interpretation decides whether it is false, nonrelevant, or relevant; evaluation compares relevant indications to the criteria; and reporting records the outcome.

questions.question: Which location callout makes a finding usable to a stranger years later?
options: '415 mm from the north end of weld W12, on the cap face'
questions.explanation: A location only survives if it is dimensioned from a named, permanent datum. Chalk marks wash off, rust gets cleaned, and 'near the top' depends on who is standing where.
